sources.notes: Count/compute modular tetration-related values over many queries.
Official editorial decomposes the modular exponent tower using multiplicative orders and divisor enumeration.

annotation.summary: Solve modular tetration queries where exponent towers must be evaluated through changing moduli.
annotation.constraints: The modulus can be large, but the recursive depth over phi/order chains is small enough with factorization and divisor enumeration.
annotation.core_idea: For a^e mod m, once gcd(a,m)=1 the exponent can be reduced modulo the multiplicative order of a mod m. If a is not coprime, split prime-power factors and handle the saturated valuation separately, then combine residues. The tetration height is handled recursively on the required exponent modulus. Counting choices introduces combinatorial factors over divisors/order classes.
annotation.complexity: Polynomial in the number of divisors/factors per query; practically O(tau(m) log m) with precomputed factorization helpers.

solution_variants.name: order-recursive-tetration
solution_variants.summary: Evaluate exponent towers recursively modulo multiplicative orders, splitting non-coprime prime powers when necessary.
solution_variants.complexity: O(tau(m) log m) style per query

tags.evidence: The central reduction is exponent modulo the multiplicative order of the base under the current modulus.
tags.description: Use multiplicative orders modulo m to reduce exponents or classify periodic powers.
tags.created_reason: Multiplicative order is a reusable number-theory technique, especially in modular exponent/tetration problems.

tags.evidence: The whole problem is built around modular exponent towers and modulus reduction.
tags.description: General modular arithmetic techniques including modular exponentiation, modulus reduction, and congruence manipulation.
tags.created_reason: Many number-theory problems use modular arithmetic without fitting a narrower tag like CRT or Mobius.

tags.evidence: The editorial counts valid exponent/order classes by divisor enumeration.
